mission.title: Install and Configure Virtual Kubelet on Kubernetes
mission.description: Virtual Kubelet is an open source Kubernetes kubelet implementation that allows Kubernetes to connect to other APIs, enabling the use of serverless container platforms like ACI and AWS Fargate. Installing Virtual Kubelet allows you to extend your Kubernetes cluster capabilities by integrating with various cloud services.

steps.title: Add the Virtual Kubelet Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Virtual Kubelet Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add virtual-kubelet https://charts.virtual-kubelet.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Virtual Kubelet using Helm
steps.description:
Install Virtual Kubelet in the `virtual-kubelet` namespace with the following command:
```bash
helm install virtual-kubelet virtual-kubelet/virtual-kubelet --namespace virtual-kubelet --create-namespace --version v1.12.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Virtual Kubelet pod is running by executing:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n virtual-kubelet
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for Virtual Kubelet
steps.description:
Update the Virtual Kubelet deployment to set resource limits. Use the following command to edit the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment virtual-kubelet -n virtual-kubelet
```
In the editor, add resource limits under the `spec.containers.resources` section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for Virtual Kubelet
steps.description:
Create a Role and RoleBinding for Virtual Kubelet to ensure it has the necessary permissions. Run the following commands:
```bash
kubectl create role virtual-kubelet-role --verb=get,list,watch,create,update,delete --resource=pods --namespace=virtual-kubelet
kubectl create rolebinding virtual-kubelet-binding --role=virtual-kubelet-role --serviceaccount=virtual-kubelet:default --namespace=virtual-kubelet
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Virtual Kubelet will show the pod running in the `virtual-kubelet` namespace when you execute `kubectl get pods -n virtual-kubelet`. Additionally, resource limits and RBAC settings should be properly configured for optimal operation.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add virtual-kubelet https://charts.virtual-kubelet.io
helm repo update
helm install virtual-kubelet virtual-kubelet/virtual-kubelet --namespace virtual-kubelet --create-namespace --version v1.12.0

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

prerequisites.description: You need a running Kubernetes cluster with Helm and kubectl installed.
